summary: The post argues that software engineers must adopt 'system-system thinking' — recognizing that the systems they build exist within larger ecological and social systems. Drawing parallels between the internet's design and natural ecosystems, the author warns that global warming and ecological collapse pose existential threats to the technology industry itself, since high technology depends on organized human civilization.
key_points: Software systems have physical manifestations through hardware and people, connecting them to larger real-world systems
The internet's success comes from design principles inspired by ecosystems, but ecosystems themselves are now collapsing
Global warming and mass extinction threaten the social infrastructure that technology depends on
Purely technical solutions like P2P systems are insufficient; the underlying social and ecological systems must be preserved
Technologists must either design systems robust against social collapse or work to maintain organized human life
money_quotes: A system-system thinker is someone who understands that the system they are building is part of a larger whole of other systems.
High technology needs organized human life to develop and function, and it won't be possible with systemic failure.
If the total collapse of our ecosystem causes mass social upheaval, the internet will stop functioning.
By understanding that our computer systems are connected to much larger systems, I don't believe in a purely technical solution anymore.
Only 4% of all mammals are wild.
key_insight: The technology industry's survival depends not on technical resilience alone but on the health of the ecological and social systems it is embedded within, making environmental collapse an existential threat to software itself.
